does: This paper introduces LITMUS, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating behavioral jailbreaks in LLM-based autonomous agents operating in real operating systems. The core contribution is a dual verification mechanism that checks safety violations at both semantic and physical (OS-level) layers, combined with OS-level state rollback to prevent test contamination. The benchmark comprises 819 test cases spanning three adversarial paradigms: jailbreak speaking, skill injection, and entity wrapping. The authors develop a fully automated multi-agent evaluation framework and conduct extensive testing across frontier agents, revealing that current systems lack effective safety awareness when executing dangerous OS operations with irreversible consequences.
connects: This work extends the safety evaluation landscape beyond content-filtering benchmarks by addressing physical-layer harms in agentic systems. It builds on prior semantic-level safety evaluation frameworks but identifies and fills a critical gap: most existing benchmarks operate only at the conversational layer and cannot detect actual OS-level harm. The paper connects to earlier work on jailbreaking language models and adversarial prompting, while pivoting focus from what agents say to what they actually do in real environments. It responds to the growing deployment of autonomous agents that interact with operating systems and the inadequacy of semantic-only safety measures to capture irreversible consequences of agent behavior.
enables: This benchmark establishes foundational infrastructure for safer deployment of autonomous agents in real OS environments. By providing systematic test cases and evaluation methodology, it enables future research on robust defenses against behavioral jailbreaks at both semantic and execution layers. The work points toward developing agents with genuine physical-world safety awareness rather than surface-level content filtering. It supports research into safety mechanisms that prevent skill injection attacks and adversarial entity wrapping, and enables evaluation of defense strategies that can withstand multiple coordinated attack paradigms. The automated framework and rollback mechanism provide tools for iterative safety improvement and could facilitate industry standards for agent safety certification.
